author: @Frossglass
text: hmmm the adsorption method seems to be the most energy efficient of all, the major problem I see is that all the common desiccants used for this water harvesting are rigid and, the only possible metod to recover the water out of them is by heating up, so that makes the process, overall less efficient. So what if instead of using rigid brittle materials like zeolites or silica gel, we use some kind of soft sponge?, so that to recover de water out, we just could have to squeeze the material?, no heating needed. I have tested the cellulose-made sponges used for electronics soldering, and that material can absorb until 20% it's own weight of water, in a 40-50% RH air, but unfortunately this is not enough to get the water out by squeezing it, so maybe an improvement in the chemical composition or an additive could do the job.

author: @javelinXH992
text:
I have small desktop humidifier in my garage to help keep moisture levels dry. It is a 23watt unit (tiny!) and is on a timer on and off as it does not need to run continuously. It is a Peltier effect device. Every weekend there is 100 to 200 ml of water in it which I then take to my car and pour in the washer bottle reservoir. This is water from the air so it contains no salts that cause hardness, it is basically distilled water. Therefore it leaves no drying stains on my car windscreen. Our tap water is quite hard. So the waste from keeping my garage dry is used for a positive purpose.

I also use some of the disposable calcium chloride dehydrators in the corners and near tools. Once full of liquid, I pour them into a sealed container and take them to work. I work for a small chemical manufacturing company. Calcium chloride is an excellent precipitant for waste metals in our effluent plant so it supplements our purchased in treatment chemicals. We’ve tried it in the lab, it works great.

So I’ve found a genuine reuse for two of my domestic waste streams. With a bit of thought and looking into things (Google it!), we can increase the amount of useful work we extract from a lot what we have already created before it is finally disposed of.

author: @AnalystPrime
text:
Yeah, there are many places where people have been using fog catcher nets and similar constructions for ages, some literally for a thousand years, and at least two modern versions exist that need power but can produce water even in desert conditions like 10% humidity. And yes, even though it would be cheaper to use a well or drink tap water, those are not available for millions of people and would take time to build. Meanwhile, even a low estimate of what one of the generators can produce compared to its cost makes the captured water far cheaper than bottled water, and that is the cost of a bottle at the corner store, not trucked to some drought stricken village in middle of a desert warzone...

I find these water generators quite mysterious, as in it is a mystery how there are people who keep saying they don't work, or are fakes that only collect rain. It made sense for people to wonder if electric cars would be possible back when li-ion batteries had not been invented yet, or if solar power would be any use when panels were 5% efficient and cost $100 per watt, but anyone claiming condensation is impossible is not just questioning some company ad campaign, they are outright denying how weather and the laws of physics work!

Few years back I heard of device called Source panel and Googled for more data; I did find the company website but the top hit was actually a fake news site pretending to be some kind of scientific journal, with fake interviews of "experts" saying that the panel does not work, buy bottled water instead. It resembles the anti-renewables and anti-EV propaganda, but there is a major difference between EVs making ICE obsolete outside very few cases, and people using water generators in locations where neither tap, well nor bottled water is available...

So, seriously, WTF is going on?

replies.author: @elvendragonhammer5433
replies.text:
Nope- you need to go back to science class cause you have that backwards- In fact Robert has already stated as much in at least one of his video's. There are only 4 ways to desalenate water. However only 3 are used because the other one is too impractical -centrifugal force- <although it is relitively fast>.

1) Normal Reverse-Osmosis: <this is the lowest-energy, but takes the longest>. This requires using a pool of "sacrificial" water, like Grey or urine/waste water & using an osmosis filter setup to migrate the salinity from one to the other. The problem is it requires twice the volume of water to "absorb" the saline as the original volume with the salt in it. In addition it can take days or even months depending on the initial volume of water, it's salinity & the ambient temperature <usually outdoors>.

2) Distillation: Requires removing as many solid particulates from the water as possible, usually by mechanical or methods similar to a waste-treatment facility. Then heating the salinated water to boiling, & then to fully turn it to steam- the higher saline content the longer & more heat is required to boil & turn it to steam.
The boiling point of normal water is 100°C or (212°F)
The boiling point of a saturated salt solution (seawater) is 108.7°C (227.7 °F).

It takes 2,260,000 Joules (2,260 KiloJoules) to convert 1 kg of liquid water at one atmosphere to 1 kg of steam at at one atmosphere to 100 ºC. (that's not factoring in teh saline content)

Then that steam will need to be condensed in some way, usually also requiring refrigeration. So it's already 2-3x as inefficiant & energy intensive as an air conditioner.

3) Forced Reverse-Osmosis: This requires both quite cold & high pressure conditions. 
The eutectic point(Just above freezing) for saline saturated water is -21.12°C (-6.02°F)
This is listed because it cannot freeze.
It would nornally be (0°C/32°F) to freeze.
Not only must it be kept at this temperature but must be under pressure of At least 10-30 atmosphere's greater than at sea level, which requires very heavy commpressor stages & equipment. It is capable of making the highest volume of drinkable water in the shortest time. But is the most energy intensive.

You have apparently never heard of a simple process called condensation. You can take a 45°F cup from your fridge & pull aprox 1/4th to 2/3rds of the same volume of water in the cold cup from the air by the time the cold cup cools- with no added energy.
you can use an air-conditioner setup to get water out of the air in just a few minutes, 

For example, in a room where the air is (27°C/80°F) and the relative humidity is 75%. Any object with a temperature of (22°C/71°F) or lower will become covered by condensation. <in other words, at 75% humidity a temperature difference of 9 degrees is all that is needed to create drinkable water.> 

I have a I have an Ice maker that makes 8 Large Ice cubes in one cycle (9 minutes) or 8 small ones in just under 5 minutes. So in one hour that's 48 Ice cubes, if all it needed to do was pull water from the air- it will start to frost over in seconds after it starts pumping freon.

I also have a 11,000BTU (1500 Wattperhour) portable air conditioner, that after about 2 hours (in about 80-85% humidity) it collects almost 5 liters in it's condensation tank. If I don't dump it every night, the next time I run it, it will overflow.
@kensmith5694

author: @davidglynnguitars8119
text: This video reminded me of something I saw a number of years ago. They buried a container and directed ambient air down into the container. The difference in temperature caused condensation and the container collected the water. They could extract the water from the container with a small pump. It worked well in hot climates because the difference in temperature underground compared to the ambient temperature above ground was enough to cause condensation. I'm sure that the pump could be powered by a small solar cell. It was very interesting.

replies.author: @oildalestar
replies.text: What you need to know is that was a proven fraud never produced anything except money for the scammer

replies.author: @davidglynnguitars8119
replies.text: @oildalestar I didn't see anyone selling it, I just watched a video showing how it was supposed to work. The idea seemed fairly sound. I never attempted to try it, but with a difference of 30-40F, I would think some condensation would occur. How much, who knows. But, people who use geothermal cooling for green houses have to use perforated tubing because solid tubing does collect water.

replies.author: @kensmith5694
replies.text: @davidglynnguitars8119 The idea *IS* a fraud.  It doesn't work.  Very quickly, the air warms the soil above dew point.  It is one of those things of physics that condensing water gives up an awful lot of heat.  Soil isn't very thermally conductive.

text: If you have ice and a cup it will condense on the outside of the cup. Now there are dew catchers. I thought of a 3d mesh screen and cooling that using the dew point of air to condense water. But instead of ice you could use a metal that is slow to change its temperature. So freezing that could cool air to condense water. If it is lead or steel, or bismuth you could put it in a copper pipe. Freeze it and put it in a aero gel to condense water.

author: @victoryfirst2878
text: ROBERT, you have the worlds sweetest imagination. This planet is a water bubble.
